title: Signs and symptoms of recurrent pericarditis
content:
Pericarditis is when the pericardium becomes inflamed. The pericardium is a sac-like structure composed of two thin layers of tissue that surround the heart, providing protection.

If a person goes 4 or 6 weeks without symptoms after their initial episode, but then experiences symptoms again, this is known as a flare. At that point, a healthcare professional may diagnose recurrent pericarditis.

The signs of recurrent pericarditis can be similar to a heart attack, so it’s important to know how to recognize them and know when to get medical attention.

The most common symptom of pericarditis is chest pain, which is present in almost all cases.

The chest pain could feel like a stabbing sensation, a dull ache, or pressure. The pain may also spread to the neck, shoulder, arm, upper back, or abdomen.

This type of pain may also become worse when lying down, coughing, or taking deep breaths.

Partially leaning forward and sitting up straight may ease the pain.

There are other symptoms to be aware of, which may help you identify a recurrent pericarditis flare. 

Chest pain is the main symptom of pericarditis. However, this can also indicate a heart attack or other health conditions that should be ruled out, so it is vital to call 911 to ensure prompt medical care.

Other reasons for chest pain include:

The inflammation, swelling, and irritation of the pericardium can cause a person to feel short of breath.

This may occur when being physically active, but may also occur when lying down.

Shortness of breath may occur due to a swollen pericardium, which restricts the heart’s ability to pump effectively and can lead to fluid buildup.

Pericarditis can cause flu-like symptoms because the inflammation may be triggered by a viral or respiratory infection or the body’s autoimmune response.

Symptoms may include:

When the body fights infection, it can take a physical toll, leading to fatigue, tiredness, and weakness.

These symptoms occur because the irregular pericardium interferes with the heart’s pumping, preventing it from pumping enough blood for the body to function optimally.

Swelling, also known as edema, occurs in pericarditis due to an inflamed, irritated pericardium, leading to fluid accumulation.

Because fluid buildup impairs the heart’s ability to pump effectively, blood backs up in the veins, leading to swelling in the legs, abdomen, and sometimes the lungs.

The swelling can also cause an irregular heartbeat.

This can be heart palpitations, which feel like a “fluttering” sensation, or a faster-than-usual heartbeat.

Symptoms of recurrent pericarditis may change with each flare.

The initial episode is typically the most severe. Subsequent flares may be milder and sometimes harder to diagnose and manage. The duration between episodes can also vary significantly from person to person.

There are occasions when diagnostic tools will reveal signs of pericarditis.

Pericarditis symptoms can feel like a heart attack or another serious health condition. If a person experiences the following, they should call 911 — or their local emergency number — and get immediate medical attention:

Signs of pericarditis can look a lot like a heart attack, so it’s important that a person gets urgent medical care if they feel chest pain or worsening symptoms.

Chest pain is the most common symptom of pericarditis, but other signs and symptoms include fatigue, fever, and flu-like symptoms.

After the initial episode, returning symptoms, known as flares, are typically milder, but individuals should still get medical care as soon as possible.

title: Signs and symptoms of Prader-Willi syndrome
content:
Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is a rare genetic disorder that produces a range of effects throughout the body, notably hypotonia during infancy and developmental delays, extreme hunger, and obesity later on. 

Symptoms of PWS vary among individuals. They may develop slowly or be difficult to notice, and they evolve as a person ages. However, there are distinct physical, behavioral, and hormonal symptoms that may develop during different stages of a person’s life.

In this article, we discuss the types of symptoms individuals with PWS may experience and detail how the condition manifests at different ages.

Diagnosis of PWS typically occurs early. In developed countries, diagnosis may occur at around age 2 months.

Infants with PWS may experience low muscle tone, or hypotonia, which can cause them to feel “floppy” when a person is holding them, according to the National Organization for Rare Disorders (NORD).

Doctors may identify hypotonia prenatally by reduced fetal movement.

Nearly all infants with PWS will experience low muscle tone. In some cases, it may be severe. Generally, hypotonia improves with age.

Signs of hypotonia in infants include:

Infants with PWS may be born with or may develop certain facial features. These include:

In children, PWS causes an increase in appetite and food-seeking behavior, along with a lack of satiety, which is a feeling of fullness after eating.

Excessive food consumption can lead to overeating, weight gain, and obesity. Some children may have an intense compulsion to eat that causes behaviors such as hoarding or food theft and may damage the gastrointestinal tract. And obesity may produce complications such as diabetes and heart disease.

NORD reports that changes in appetite may become noticeable between the ages of 2 and 4.5 years. Increases in calorie intake may start before age 8 years. Other sources suggest that excessive eating may begin between the ages of 6 and 12 years.

In addition to changes in appetite and eating, children experience a range of cognitive and hormonal symptoms related to dysfunction in a part of the brain called the hypothalamus.

Physical symptoms of PWS include the altered facial features described above. Other possible symptoms include smaller hands and feet. However, this may not be observable until after infancy.

Children with PWS may present with a range of behavioral and cognitive symptoms.

PWS can lead to cognitive impairment in the form of lower-than-typical intelligence the respective age group, learning difficulties, or delayed intellectual development. It can also lead to slower language acquisition and delayed walking.

Most children with PWS experience behavioral challenges such as:

A common hormonal symptom of PWS is hypogonadism, which involves reduced function of the sex glands (testes in males and ovaries in females).

With hypogonadism, the sex glands produce lower amounts of hormones. Low production of sex hormones can cause:

Another hormonal symptom of PWS is growth hormone (GH) deficiency. During adolescence, growth hormone stimulates bone and cartilage growth. A child with GH deficiency may display:

Early treatment of GH deficiency — ideally during infancy — can effectively increase height and muscle mass and prevent a range of other complications from GH deficiency.

PWS occurs most often when a certain gene on the paternal chromosome 15 is either missing or deleted during fetal development.

These genetic changes cause dysfunction in a part of the brain called the hypothalamus. The hypothalamus controls bodily processes such as:

When the hypothalamus is not working properly, it throws off these processes and causes many of the symptoms characteristic of PWS. Because the function of the hypothalamus changes as a person ages, the associated symptoms also change.

It is best to speak with a doctor if a child displays any of the clinical symptoms of PWS, such as:

If a doctor suspects PWS, they may recommend a DNA test to confirm or rule out the condition. These analyses are at least 99% successful in detecting the condition.

Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is a rare genetic disorder that creates a range of symptoms throughout the body. These symptoms evolve as a person ages.

During infancy, a baby may display low muscle tone (hypotonia), developmental delays, and feeding difficulties. As a child ages, their hypotonia resolves, and they begin displaying an excessive appetite, which can lead to obesity.

From adolescence onward, PWS presents intellectual disabilities, hypogonadism, and behavior issues, in addition to excessive eating.

title: Understanding hives and fever: Causes and treatments
content:
Hives and fever may occur together as a response to a viral or bacterial infection. While hives are a skin reaction, a fever indicates that the body is fighting a more widespread infection.

Hives, also known as urticaria, are itchy, raised welts on the skin that can change shape or move around. They are a common skin reaction, but do not usually cause a fever. 

When a fever accompanies hives, it is usually the body’s systemic response to an illness, such as a cold, flu, or strep throat.

A fever itself does not typically cause hives. However, the two symptoms may share a common trigger, usually a viral infection.

In these cases, the fever is the body’s immune response to the virus, while the hives are a skin reaction to the same infection. This is particularly common in children.

Conversely, typical acute urticaria or short-term hives is usually an isolated skin condition and does not present with a fever. If a person has hives and a fever, it strongly suggests a whole-body, or systemic issue, rather than a localized skin allergy.

Hives are itchy bumps on the skin appearing raised. On lighter skin, they typically appear red or pink. On darker skin, they may be the same color as the surrounding skin or slightly darker. They can turn pale when pressed, though this change can be harder to see on dark skin.

These welts occur when the body releases histamine and other chemicals into the blood, causing small blood vessels to leak fluid. This reaction can occur for various reasons, including allergies, infections, stress, or skin pressure.

The primary signs of hives and fever are the physical appearance of hives, accompanied by an elevated body temperature.

Signs may include:

Most cases of hives accompanied by a mild fever are not dangerous and typically resolve on their own. However, certain symptoms require medical attention to rule out severe allergic reactions or serious infections.

A person should contact a doctor if:

Seek emergency medical attention if signs of anaphylaxis appear, including:

When hives and fever occur together, it is usually due to an infection or an underlying systemic condition.

Common causes include:

Treatment focuses on making the individual comfortable and addressing the underlying cause.

Treatment options include:

The outlook for hives and fever is generally good. When from a viral infection, both symptoms typically resolve on their own within a few days to a week.

Hives from an allergic reaction or medication may disappear once the trigger is gone, though they can sometimes persist for a few weeks. If the cause is a chronic autoimmune condition, the symptoms may fluctuate over a prolonged period and necessitate long-term management.

Preventing hives and fever primarily involves avoiding infections and known allergens.

Strategies include:

Hives and fever are distinct symptoms, but they can appear together, usually due to a viral or bacterial infection. The fever occurs as the immune system fights the illness, and the hives develop as a reaction to that process.

Most cases are mild and resolve with rest, fluids, and over-the-counter medications to manage symptoms such as itching and fever. However, if symptoms are severe, persistent, or accompanied by breathing difficulties, medical attention is necessary.

title: What causes menopause, and can I delay it?
content:
Menopause occurs when a person stops having periods due to a decline in certain hormones. There is no medical treatment to delay menopause, but treatment can help people manage any symptoms. 

Some research suggests that certain lifestyle factors may help postpone the onset of menopause, but more clinical evidence is necessary.

Menopause begins with a transitional phase in which a person’s menstrual cycle patterns start to change. It ends after their period permanently stops. The process can take several years. A person reaches menopause once they have not experienced a period for 12 consecutive months.

Various factors may influence the age at which menopause starts, including a person’s genetics, lifestyle, and medical history. 

This article looks at what causes menopause and the hormonal changes that occur during the transition. It also discusses the factors that influence menopause, the phases of menopausal transition, and when to speak with a doctor.

Natural menopause is a normal phase of aging that indicates the end of the reproductive years for people who have periods.

The transition occurs due to a decline in certain hormones and ovarian follicles, which are fluid-filled sacs within the ovaries that contain immature eggs. Estrogen and other hormones decline at the same time.

The decrease in estrogen causes the lining of the uterus, called the endometrium, to stop thickening in preparation for pregnancy and shedding during menstruation. This results in a person’s menstrual cycle becoming irregular until their period eventually stops permanently. 

Menopause usually starts between ages 45 and 55. The average age of onset in the United States is age 52. 

As a person ages and enters menopause, the number of granulosa cells in their ovaries declines. These cells produce and secrete various hormones, including:

As levels of these hormones decline during menopause, FSH production increases. However, without enough ovarian follicles left to respond to the effects of the increasing FSH, ovulation does not occur. In turn, the ovaries produce less estrogen, endometrium development halts, and menstruation ceases.

Hormonal changes due to menopause can cause symptoms and changes in body composition. These include:

Menopausal hormone therapy (MHT), sometimes called hormone replacement therapy (HRT), can replace ovarian hormones during menopause, which helps relieve many of the symptoms. However, it cannot delay the onset of menopause.

Genes can influence menopause in different ways, including the time of onset.

People with a family history of early menopause are more likely to experience menopause before age 45.

Parents may also pass down genes for various health conditions that could increase the risk of early menopause. These include:

Variants in specific genes may also affect menopause.

Research suggests a variant in the tachykinin receptor 3 (TACR3) gene may sometimes play a role in the age of menopause onset and some menopause symptoms, including hot flashes and night sweats.

Certain health conditions and medical treatments may also contribute to or cause early menopause. These include:

Chronic inflammation may decrease the number of cells in the ovaries that produce estrogen and other hormones, and cause other structural and functional changes that may contribute to ovarian aging. 

This could result in early menopause, or premature menopause, which happens before age 40.

Medical and surgical causes of menopause include:

A person may want to contact a doctor if they experience symptoms of menopause that affect their quality of life.

Hormone treatment may help a person manage symptoms like hot flashes, night sweats, and mental health difficulties.

A doctor may also be able to help reduce the risks of certain health concerns associated with menopause, including osteoporosis.

In terms of when is the right time to talk with a doctor about hormone treatment, I generally recommend the sooner the better for several reasons. 

Firstly, why suffer if you can feel better? Secondly, the most rapid bone loss occurs within the first 2 years following menopause. Lastly, hormone replacement therapy (HRT), also called MHT, is most beneficial overall in the first 10 years following menopause.

— Barry I Aron, MD, FACOG, MSCP

Read about the uses, types, and effects of HRT.

Quotes represent the opinions of our medical experts. All content is strictly informational and should not be considered medical advice.

Some lifestyle factors contribute to early menopause. People who smoke may be more likely to have early menopause and experience more severe symptoms than nonsmokers.

Scientists also associate factors like diet, exercise, and body mass index (BMI) with early menopause.

Some lifestyle factors contribute to early menopause. People who smoke may be more likely to have early menopause and experience more severe symptoms than nonsmokers.

Scientists also associate factors like diet, exercise, and body mass index (BMI) with early menopause.

Various genetic and environmental factors may contribute to premature menopause. These include family history, medical and surgical interventions, and certain health conditions.

Various genetic and environmental factors may contribute to premature menopause. These include family history, medical and surgical interventions, and certain health conditions.

People’s experiences of menopause vary widely. Some experience no or mild symptoms, while others have severe symptoms that significantly affect their quality of life. The duration of symptoms also varies between individuals.

People’s experiences of menopause vary widely. Some experience no or mild symptoms, while others have severe symptoms that significantly affect their quality of life. The duration of symptoms also varies between individuals.

There is no definitive way to delay menopause, and a person cannot prevent it.

Some research suggests the following may contribute to later-than-average natural menopause, or reduce the risk of early menopause:

There is no definitive way to delay menopause, and a person cannot prevent it.

Some research suggests the following may contribute to later-than-average natural menopause, or reduce the risk of early menopause:

Natural menopause is a normal phase of aging that follows the reproductive years in people who have periods. It occurs when ovarian follicles and certain hormones decrease, and leads to the end of menstruation.

There is no definitive way to delay menopause, although some research suggests diet and other modifiable factors may influence the time of onset. However, more research is still necessary.

Treatment can help people manage the symptoms of menopause if they occur. If a person experiences any symptoms that affect their quality of life, they can contact a doctor.

title: Achondroplasia management and treatment options
content:
Achondroplasia is a form of short-limbed dwarfism characterized by physical features such as reduced adult height, an enlarged head, distinct facial features, and shortening of the upper part of the limbs.

Comprehensive management of achondroplasia is important throughout an individual’s life span, as the condition increases the risk of various complications related to disproportionate skeletal growth.

In this article, we discuss achondroplasia management, covering treatment goals, medications, surgeries, management of complications, long-term monitoring, and emerging therapies.

There is no cure for achondroplasia, so treatment involves a multidisciplinary approach to management from infancy through adulthood.

Achondroplasia increases the risk of specific complications during different phases of a person’s life.

For example, infants and toddlers are at risk of foramen magnum stenosis, a narrowing of the base of the skull where the brainstem meets the spinal cord. Adults are at risk of spinal stenosis, which is a narrowing of the spinal canal.

Ongoing surveillance by specialists can help identify issues early and lead to prompt treatment.

While the standard approach to treatment involves monitoring the condition over time and addressing any complications that may arise, in recent years, scientists have developed medications to promote growth by addressing the underlying genetic cause of the condition.

In 2021, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved vosoritide (Voxzogo), the first drug specifically indicated for the treatment of achondroplasia. This medication promotes linear limb growth. Studies have found that vosoritide leads to an additional 1.57 centimeters of height growth each year.

Researchers are still investigating the effectiveness of vosoritide in preventing complications associated with achondroplasia. Research suggests it may prevent tibial bowing (curving of the lower leg), which is a common source of pain and dysfunction among individuals with the condition.

It is still unclear what effect the drug may have on other bone-related complications, such as foramen magnum stenosis and spinal stenosis. However, researchers are optimistic about its potential in this area.

Limb lengthening surgery works by cutting the bones of the upper arms or legs and using external braces or magnets to gradually draw the bones apart.

Although medications offer individuals with achondroplasia a pharmacological route for increasing height, surgery remains an option.

By increasing height, surgery has the potential to improve a person’s quality of life and ability to perform activities of daily living. However, it carries risks and is a lengthy process.

Limb lengthening remains a controversial option. A 2025 study of 467 people with achondroplasia who underwent limb lengthening and their caregivers revealed a broad range of perspectives on the procedure.

Only 23% of these individuals said they would recommend the surgery to others, while 28% said they would not recommend it.

Due to the risk of developing complications from achondroplasia, healthcare professionals recommend routine monitoring throughout an individual’s life.

A multidisciplinary team, including pediatricians, geneticists, neurologists, orthopedists, and more, will evaluate an individual at different stages, evaluating bodily systems and monitoring for specific health concerns.

Experts suggest a general evaluation at the following frequencies:

In addition to these general checkups, healthcare professionals will evaluate for the following complications at different intervals:

If healthcare professionals suspect a complication, they will refer the individual to an appropriate specialist, as needed, to ensure proper management.

Different complications have different potential treatments. Below are some potential approaches to managing common complications of achondroplasia:

While vosoritide was the first drug approved by the FDA for achondroplasia, researchers are exploring the potential of a range of other medications.

Vosoritide is a modified analogue of a hormone called C-type natriuretic peptide (CNP). This hormone plays a role in stimulating bone growth.

Researchers have developed navepegritide (Yuviwel), a long-acting CNP drug that aims to provide the growth plates with more consistent levels of CNP. This drug, which is administered via injection under the skin, received FDA approval in February 2026.

A drug called RMB-007 is currently under development. It works by blocking a protein that contributes to the development of dwarfism.

Achondroplasia management combines lifelong supportive care with targeted therapies and possible surgery.

Consistent partnership with a multidisciplinary care team is the best way to address complications early and improve outcomes.

title: Managing fatigue during lymphoma treatment
content:
• Cancer-related fatigue (CRF) is a prevalent issue for individuals undergoing lymphoma treatment, stemming from the cancer itself, the emotional and physical toll, and the side effects of treatment.• Effective management of CRF involves a combination of strategies, including energy conservation, adjusting expectations, light exercise, symptom tracking, delegating tasks, medication adjustments, and supportive care.• Adopting healthy daily habits such as balanced nutrition, adequate hydration, good sleep hygiene, and avoiding unhealthy behaviors can significantly help in reducing the impact of fatigue during lymphoma treatment.

Lymphoma is a broad term for cancers that originate in the lymphatic system. It includes two primary types: Hodgkin lymphoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. These are distinguished by differences in their cell structures.

All lymphomas involve immune cells called lymphocytes. When these cells become cancerous, they can grow and spread uncontrollably, crowding out healthy cells.

Cancer-related fatigue (CRF) is common at all stages of lymphoma. It can affect people for different reasons. For some, CRF is a symptom brought on by cancer-related changes in the body. For others, it is the result of cancer’s emotional and physical toll. Fatigue can also be a side effect of lymphoma treatment.

For many people, CRF is a combination of all of the above. A large, global patient survey from 2024 found the majority of people living with lymphoma experience fatigue as both a symptom and a treatment side effect.

It is not always possible to eliminate CRF entirely, but management strategies can help.

This article takes a look at ways to reduce fatigue and its impact during lymphoma treatment.

CRF can range in severity. Some days, it might be a persistent feeling of tiredness. On other days, it may be so intense that it limits basic activities such as climbing stairs, showering, or grocery shopping.

Signs of CRF to watch for can include:

CRF can resemble depression, and it is possible for CRF and depression to occur at the same time.

Managing fatigue during lymphoma treatment can be a process of trial and error. Each person is different, and what works for one might not work for another. Some people may need to adopt several CRF management strategies to notice a difference.

Conserving energy means not demanding too much from the body at a single time. Expending energy in small doses helps lower the chance of reaching exhaustion. 

Ways to conserve energy include:

Frustration, sadness, and other negative emotions can build when CRF prevents typical function. Adjusting expectations can help reduce how often those emotions take over.

Adjusting expectations can mean:

It may seem counterintuitive to exercise, but research suggests that exercise does not worsen CRF. In fact, light exercise supports physical endurance, boosts mood, and helps improve energy.

Exercise programs vary based on a person’s diagnosis, overall health, age, and preference. General recommendations are for:

Tracking symptoms is a strategy that helps reveal patterns and triggers of CRF so they can be avoided.

Examples of details to include in a symptom tracker are:

It is OK to ask for help. CRF is a valid and real experience that is not always improved by resting. 

Delegating can look like:

Educating others about CRF and why it happens can help them remain empathetic and willing to take on additional responsibilities at home, school, or work.

Doctors can make medication adjustments to help with CRF. These changes may include new dosing instructions on current medications or the addition of new medications that promote alertness and wakefulness.

CRF can be compounded by challenges such as pain, depression, and other chronic health issues. Supportive care focuses on the overall factors contributing to fatigue. Doctors may recommend:

Daily habits play an important role in overall health and well-being in lymphoma. Improvements to daily living can help the body stay strong and recover more efficiently from cancer therapies.

A healthy, balanced diet provides the body with adequate nutrients to repair tissues, maintain strength, and support immune function. In lymphoma, a healthy diet lays the foundation for physical and mental resilience.

Staying hydrated means consuming enough water to support the body’s needs. When a person does not get enough water through eating or drinking, they become dehydrated.

Dehydration can change how well cells and tissues function in the body. It can slow metabolism and promote fatigue, weakness, and poor concentration.

Dehydration can reduce blood volume and lead to low blood pressure, which may contribute to fatigue.

Cancer treatments can disrupt sleep, which reduces the body’s ability to recover and can affect mental health. Tips to improve sleep during lymphoma treatment include:

Certain unhealthy habits can make it more difficult for the body to recover from lymphoma treatments.

Habits like smoking, drinking alcohol, and substance misuse can compound inflammation, promote oxidative stress, and cause cellular damage in the body.

Cancer-related fatigue is common during lymphoma treatment. It can be caused by cancer’s effects on the body, the emotional and physical toll of living with cancer, and cancer treatments themselves.

It may not be possible to completely eliminate CRF, but management strategies can help. Conserving energy, asking for help, monitoring symptoms, and prioritizing a healthy lifestyle are just a few ways to combat fatigue.

title: Is rheumatoid arthritis an autoimmune disease?
content:
Yes, rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disease. It occurs when a person’s immune system attacks the body’s tissues, damaging their joints.

In RA, immune cells cause inflammation in the synovium, the lining of a person’s joints. The autoimmune process may begin years before a person develops symptoms and may start elsewhere in the body before presenting in the joints.

According to the United Kingdom’s National Health Service (NHS), it is unclear exactly what triggers the immune system to do this in a person with RA. However, the interaction of genetic and environmental factors may be the cause.

Certain people, such as those with a family history of RA, may have a higher risk of developing the condition.

However, if someone has repeated exposure to an environmental trigger, their immune response may also repeatedly activate and trigger the development of certain antibodies typically associated with RA.

Although there is no single test to diagnose RA, blood tests may allow healthcare professionals to look for certain autoimmune markers and antibodies that signal the condition. 

For example, many people with RA have antibodies called rheumatoid factors in their blood. This antibody is a type of protein that the immune system typically uses to protect the body.

Doctors may also test for cyclic citrullinated antibody (CCP Ab), which is a more specific autoantibody for RA.

Not everyone with RA will have high levels of rheumatoid factors, and some people without RA can have high levels. Therefore, doctors will use a variety of diagnostic steps to diagnose a person, not just one. 

To treat RA, healthcare professionals may prescribe immunosuppressant medications, such as disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) and biologics.

Immunosuppressant drugs suppress parts of the immune system to slow or stop it from attacking a person’s body. This can help to reduce inflammation in RA and prevent or slow further joint damage.

Different immunosuppressant drugs have different effects on a person’s immune system. People with RA can speak with their healthcare team if they want to learn more about the options available to them.

title: Is there a cure for rheumatoid arthritis?
content:
Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) does not have a cure, and is a progressive condition, which means it continually develops over time. 

Treatment for RA may help to slow the condition’s progression and aims to prevent irreversible joint damage. People with RA who do not have treatment may experience disability and a less favorable outlook.

Research suggests that around 40% of people with RA have functional disability that affects their daily lives and ability to work within 10 years of a diagnosis.

Seeking early treatment, which means within six months of first experiencing symptoms, can improve a person’s joint functioning and may lead to lower levels of swollen, tender joints. It also decreases the risk of bone erosions.

Early treatment can also increase a person’s chance of remission, a period in which they have no signs or symptoms of the disease. Some people may choose to slowly decrease treatment if they achieve remission.

However, even late treatment can offer benefits and significantly improve a person’s outlook. People with RA tend to have similar mortality rates if they have treatment, whether it is early or later.

Healthcare professionals may recommend a combination of treatments for someone with RA. A person’s treatment plan may change over time as their condition progresses. Treatment goals tend to be:

A doctor may recommend the following treatment options:

People with RA also benefit from regular exercise, which may involve creating a tailored exercise plan, stress reduction, and dietary changes.

A person’s healthcare team will continually monitor them and adjust the treatment plan when necessary. It is important for someone with RA to attend regular check-ups, which may involve blood tests, imaging tests, bone health assessments, and more.

People with RA may also develop complications that require treatment and affect a person’s quality of life and outlook. Common complications of RA include the following:

Anyone who develops symptoms of RA can benefit from speaking with a healthcare professional to create a treatment plan as quickly as possible.

People with RA can also benefit from speaking with their healthcare team if they develop new symptoms or challenges with their daily function. A doctor may suggest making changes to their current treatment plan.
